description:
You are an on-call operations engineer. 1. Harden the incident directory so that it uses the set-gid mechanism and sensible group permissions.
2. Normalise the permissions of an existing log file, create a new “flag” file that marks the end of triage, and finally produce an *audit log* that records the effective octal permissions of every regular file in the directory. • A directory already exists at /home/user/incident_reports.
• Inside it there is exactly **one** file to start with: /home/user/incident_reports/report_2023_11_15.txt (its current permissions may be anything).
• You do NOT have root privileges; work only inside /home/user.
• Use standard GNU/Linux user-space tools already available in a minimal container (bash, coreutils, etc.). A. Directory permissions • Set /home/user/incident_reports to octal **2775** (rwx rws r-x, i.e. set-gid + group-writable + group-executable).
B. Existing file permissions • Ensure report_2023_11_15.txt ends up with octal **660** (rw-rw----). Do **not** change its contents.
C. New “flag” file • Create an *empty* file called triage_complete.flag in the same directory.
• Set its permissions to octal **640** (rw-r-----).
D. Permission audit log • Create /home/user/incident_reports/permissions_audit.log.
• It must contain **exactly two lines**, one per regular file currently present.
Format (strict, no extra spaces or trailing blanks): <filename><single space><octal_permission>
• The expected final content of the audit log is literally: 
report_2023_11_15.txt 660
triage_complete.flag 640
• The file must end with a single trailing newline.
 Automated tests will verify: 1. Mode of /home/user/incident_reports is **2775** (set-gid bit set). 2. Mode of report_2023_11_15.txt is **660**. 3. Mode of triage_complete.flag is **640** and the file is empty. 4. permissions_audit.log exists and its content matches the exact two-line spec above with a single newline character at the end and no extra lines or spaces. Although the work can be completed with as few as three or four shell commands, you are free to use as many intermediate checks (ls, stat, etc.) as you deem helpful. Only the final state matters.
truth:
<!-- Initial setup performed by the test harness -->
    <initial_state>
        Directory: /home/user/incident_reports          (mode 0755)  
        File:      /home/user/incident_reports/report_2023_11_15.txt  
                   Contents: "PLACEHOLDER: do not modify\n"  
                   Mode: 0644
    </initial_state>

    <!-- Expected final state after agent finishes -->
    <expected_final_state>
        Directory: /home/user/incident_reports  
            Mode (octal): 2775
            Set-gid bit:  yes

        File #1: /home/user/incident_reports/report_2023_11_15.txt  
            Mode (octal): 660  
            Size/contents: unchanged (still "PLACEHOLDER: do not modify\n")

        File #2: /home/user/incident_reports/triage_complete.flag  
            Mode (octal): 640  
            Size: 0 bytes (empty)

        File #3: /home/user/incident_reports/permissions_audit.log  
            Mode (octal): 644  (default is acceptable; the test will not enforce a mode)  
            Exact textual content including terminating newline:  
            "report_2023_11_15.txt 660\ntriage_complete.flag 640\n"
    </expected_final_state>
name: task_000000_3a08bb12
